items.q: Why do tropical cyclones only form over warm tropical oceans?
items.correct: They need sea surface temperatures above ~27 degrees C to a depth of 50m to provide energy through evaporation, plus enough Coriolis effect (away from the equator) to spin.
items.misconception: Hurricanes form wherever there are strong winds over the sea.
items.random_wrong: Hurricanes form when two cold fronts collide above the ocean.
items.why: Wind doesn't create hurricanes — warm ocean does. They draw energy from latent heat of evaporating seawater; that's why they weaken rapidly over land or cool water.

items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils typically infertile?
items.correct: Heavy rainfall leaches nutrients, and most nutrients are stored in living biomass and rapidly recycled — the soil itself holds very little.
items.misconception: Because so much vegetation grows there, the soil must be fertile.
items.random_wrong: Because rainforest soils are made of clay.
items.why: The lush vegetation hides poor soil. The rainforest works as a tightly closed nutrient cycle; once cleared, the system collapses quickly because the soil cannot sustain growth.

items.q: Why does the tropical rainforest have distinct vertical layers?
items.correct: Competition for light: emergents (40m+), canopy (capturing most light), understorey (shade-tolerant) and forest floor (sparse vegetation due to low light) — each adapted to its light level.
items.misconception: Rainforest layers exist because different animals live at different heights.
items.random_wrong: Rainforest layers exist because trees grow in age order tallest to shortest.
items.why: Light availability drives the stratification; animal niches are a consequence. Students reverse cause and effect — the structure is plant-competition-driven, with fauna adapting to existing layers.

items.q: What minimum sea surface temperature is needed for tropical storms?
items.why: It's 27 degrees C. “20 degrees C” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the calm centre of a tropical storm called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Eye. “Eyewall” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Storms form between ~5 and how many degrees latitude?
items.why: The real figure is 30. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
